coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries are increasingly deployed across a range of energy storage applications, heightening the need to understand failure mechanisms such as thermal runaway. This study presents a new experimental platform focusing on optical accessibility to quantify the transient ejection and ignition dynamics from a single 18650-format cell with an NMC-811 cathode. High-speed optical diagnostics, including luminosity imaging, spectral emission, photodetection, and sound measurements, were employed to characterise the spatiotemporal evolution of ejected fragments and flaming ignition. Light and sound data are temporally correlated, demonstrating that ignition is followed by flame propagation that occurs rapidly and intermittently, with significant temporal variability despite consistent thermal runaway initiation temperatures. A gaseous ignition mode is identified where solid fragments ejected from the battery coated with metallic lithium ignite pyrophorically, acting as an ignition kernel for the flammable gaseous effluent. Atomic emission of neutral lithium (Li I) at 611 nm and 671 nm is detected during ignition and flaming ignition and is hypothesised to form via lithium plating or thermal decomposition of LiC6 during thermal runaway. Atomic emission of sodium and potassium is also found, all of which is superimposed on the grey body distribution from luminous ejecta. In addition, the high-speed images are cross-correlated to provide time-resolved velocity fields and their statistical distributions of ejected particles during the entire thermal runaway event. Axial velocities possess a log-normal distribution, with peak velocities as large as 35 m s−1. This work sets the platform for subsequent studies employing high-speed optical diagnostics to deliver a quantitative database to enable modelling of Li-ion battery thermal runaway and to understand flame-induced heating of adjacent cells in a battery module.

(For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Temperature-time histories for the copper cell holder from Series 1 and Series 2 tests. Data acquisition for S1-C2 had malfunctioned just before thermal runaway; thus, a failure temperature cannot be extracted.

High temporal resolution sequences of the three distinct ignition modes (a–e) identified in the thermal runaway of the cells. Images are selected from the Photron SA-X2 camera from the Series 2 experiments.

High-speed photodiode responses (normalised) for (a) Series 1 and (b) Series 2 cells. Series 2 cells in (b) are paired with time-traces for the integrated high-speed camera pixel intensity and (c) the measured sound pressure level (SPL).
Time-evolution of the visible optical emission spectral signature during thermal runaway for S1-C2, with distinct atomic emission lines highlighted.
Two-dimensional fields of (a) mean velocity, and (b) RMS velocity, of selected ensemble time windows for S2-C1 during thermal runaway.

head.ce:title: Analysis of ejecta ignition and velocity from 18650 Li-ion battery cells during thermal runaway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries are increasingly deployed across a range of energy storage applications, heightening the need to understand failure mechanisms such as thermal runaway. This study presents a new experimental platform focusing on optical accessibility to quantify the transient ejection and ignition dynamics from a single 18650-format cell with an NMC-811 cathode. High-speed optical diagnostics, including luminosity imaging, spectral emission, photodetection, and sound measurements, were employed to characterise the spatiotemporal evolution of ejected fragments and flaming ignition. Light and sound data are temporally correlated, demonstrating that ignition is followed by flame propagation that occurs rapidly and intermittently, with significant temporal variability despite consistent thermal runaway initiation temperatures. A gaseous ignition mode is identified where solid fragments ejected from the battery coated with metallic lithium ignite pyrophorically, acting as an ignition kernel for the flammable gaseous effluent. Atomic emission of neutral lithium (Li I) at 611 nm and 671 nm is detected during ignition and flaming ignition and is hypothesised to form via lithium plating or thermal decomposition of LiC6 during thermal runaway. Atomic emission of sodium and potassium is also found, all of which is superimposed on the grey body distribution from luminous ejecta. In addition, the high-speed images are cross-correlated to provide time-resolved velocity fields and their statistical distributions of ejected particles during the entire thermal runaway event. Axial velocities possess a log-normal distribution, with peak velocities as large as 35 m s−1. This work sets the platform for subsequent studies employing high-speed optical diagnostics to deliver a quantitative database to enable modelling of Li-ion battery thermal runaway and to understand flame-induced heating of adjacent cells in a battery module.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need to address rising CO2 emissions from the power generation and transport sectors has redefined sustainability objectives in public policy. The resultant rapid electrification and hybridisation of power conversion systems has led to the urgent need for efficient energy storage. Lithium-ion (Li-ion) batteries have emerged as a leading energy storage solution compared to other alternatives. Li-ion batteries offer advantages such as reasonable mass and volumetric energy density, duty cycling performance, thermal operability range, and portability. Despite the vast market proliferation of Li-ion batteries in energy storage systems and electric vehicles, the risk of thermal runaway failure has proven to carry enormous safety and financial consequences. Thermal runaway in a single cell can encompass rapid self-heating, cell rupture, and sudden ejection of toxic combustible gases and aerosols [1]. Thermal runaway is preceded by early safety venting of the vaporised electrolyte solution inside the battery cell, which relieves the internal cell pressure. However, safety venting is insufficient to prevent thermal runaway if sufficient internal cell heating persists. In a battery storage system, the heat transfer from the hot exothermic cell, during and after thermal runaway, along with both the sensible and chemical release from the ejected effluent and any resulting flames, can preheat neighbouring cells, leading to a cascading failure sequence that can result in a large fire and/or explosion hazard [2,3].

Earlier work on the thermal runaway of Li-ion batteries primarily focused on understanding the critical thresholds for cell failure modes [2] under various battery cell parameters such as cathode chemistry [4–6], state-of-charge [5,7], electrolyte solvent flammability [8,9], cell form factor [10–12], boundary conditions [13–17], and heating configurations [18]. These works have primarily consisted of global measurements of the cell temperature [19], mass loss rate [20], heat release rate [21–23], ex-situ quantification of exhaust gas emissions [24–26], and particles [27]. Such measurements have proven useful in characterising the key parameters defining the onset of thermal runaway and overall energetics. However, in situ measurements of the venting, ignition, and flame propagation processes remain scarce in the literature. Quantifying these phenomena is critical to understanding the sensible and chemical energy released from the particle-laden effluent and resultant flames released from a cell, which can influence the heat transfer to adjacent cells in a battery module [28,29].

Spatially characterising the complex fluid dynamics and combustion phenomena during thermal runaway requires an optically accessible setup with diagnostics enabling high temporal resolution, which has only been undertaken by a few groups. A few recent studies have used high-speed line-of-sight luminescence, Schlieren imaging, and continuous-wave laser sheet illumination with an explicit focus on the initial liquid and gas venting processes upon thermal runaway initiation [13,14,30–34]. Optical measurements of single-cells from the UPV group have provided new insight into the initial gas and particle venting during the incipient moments before thermal runaway initiation, and include spray and particle velocity, cone angle, and penetration distance [14,35,36]. However, a complete spatial and temporal characterisation of the particle ejection, ignition, and flame propagation mechanism during thermal runaway remains elusive and forms a key gap. It is unknown how the characteristic jet flames generated during thermal runaway are stabilised, and whether an analogy to the auto-ignition of turbulent lifted jet flames can be drawn. Such flames are stabilised through hot co-flow products and the formation of auto-ignition kernels upstream of the flame base [37]. However, both high-speed and precise diagnostics are needed to interrogate such phenomena. Thus far, detailed investigations into the combustion processes in Li-ion battery fires are exclusive to numerical calculations, which usually model the gas generation kinetics and combustion and employ a turbulent model approach to the flow field [38]. Heat generation is treated as a chemical source term resulting from the combustion of effluent gases [38–41]. However, there is seldom consideration of the particle releases and ignition modes, mainly owing to a lack of adequate data. Validation and verification of such modelling approaches require canonical experiments and high-fidelity data with sufficient spatial and temporal resolution.

This work aims to fill the existing gap in understanding ignition modes and particle ejecta dynamics by developing a high-speed optical diagnostic platform that provides the complete time history of the thermal runaway process and the controlling ignition mechanism resulting in flaming combustion. A novel experimental setup has been developed and expanded upon an original study by the authors to provide the first time-resolved and joint quantification of the ejecta velocity fields and the spectral emissions signature [42]. The experimental platform enables extensive optical accessibility to the ejected effluent released from single 18650 battery cells with Nickel Manganese Cobalt (NMC-811) cathodes. The state of charge for each cell is 100 %, and external heating is applied to induce thermal runaway in the cell. The ejected particles and flames are measured using high-speed imaging, high-speed photodetectors, sound pressure, and visible spectral emission imaging to provide quantitative insights into the ignition modes, in-situ particle species, velocity fields and light emission. Quantitative measurements of velocity fields and a detailed understanding of the ignition mechanism are key towards developing near-field CFD models for Li-ion batteries to reliably predict the ignition and flame propagation behaviour released from cells. Such advances in models will help build safer battery pack systems and mitigation technologies. The data and conclusions presented in this paper set the platform for a more detailed investigation to exploit joint high-speed filtered imaging and laser-based diagnostic techniques.

An experimental rig (Fig. 1 ) has been constructed to deliver controlled thermal boundary conditions and optical access to the battery cell burst disc using a copper slug approach [21]. The rig is comprised of three key components: (1) copper slug cell holder, (2) mineral wool insulation layer, and (3) resistance band heaters/hot plate. The copper slug consists of two polished semi-cylinders (total diameter 57.2 mm) with an inner bore of 18.1 mm. Two ferrous pins fit into the semi-cylinders to allow a flush fit. The total mass of the copper holder is 1.27 kg. The high thermal conductivity of copper ensures that nearly isothermal conditions are achieved inside the copper cell holder during the heating phase. As the holder mass is two orders of magnitude greater than a typical 18650 nickel-plated stainless steel-cased cell, it can be assumed that the cell is in thermal equilibrium with the copper holder. Therefore, monitoring the copper slug holder temperature approximates the surface temperature of the cell before thermal runaway occurs. The near isothermal condition between the battery casing and copper heating slug eliminates the need to attach thermocouples directly onto the battery cell itself, thereby enabling complete optical accessibility to the exit plane of the battery cell at the burst disc. Boreholes are located at various locations on the cylinder, with two radial depths positioned at 5 mm and 17.5 mm to monitor the cell temperature using two 1.5 mm Inconel sheathed K-type thermocouples. Extensive testing of the copper cell holder has shown that the temperature variance across the radial and axial planes is minimal (<1 °C) and that isothermal conditions are obtained.

Lithium Nickel Manganese Cobalt oxide (LiNi1/3Mn1/3Co1/3O2, or NMC-811) 18650 form factor battery cells (Samsung INR18650-25R) were tested in this study. Each battery cell was charged to 100 % state-of-charge (SOC). The cell weight before and after thermal runaway was measured, along with the cell voltage and internal resistance before each test. The initial weight for all cells was consistent at 42.4 ± 0.36 g. The battery cell is covered by a thin layer of high-conductivity aluminium foil before insertion to ensure excellent thermal contact with the copper slug.

The vent cap (located on the positive terminal) was manually removed after charging to the desired SOC (100 %), thus leaving the thin burst disc exposed [43]. This was undertaken to facilitate a uniform boundary condition at the exit plane that was not dependent on the cell orientation relative to the optical diagnostics and vent cap design, where the number of vent holes is manufacturer-dependent. Noting that the main purpose of this work is to identify the ejecta dynamics and ignition behaviour, the removal of the vent cap prevents possible blockage of the holes due to molten slag, thereby allowing for the key phenomena to be identified more easily. Removing the vent cap also allows for a narrow cone angle of effluent to be released, rather than orthogonal jets observed by Garcia et al. [14], which are more favourable for line-of-sight optical measurements. The authors acknowledge that the removal of the vent cap will inherently influence the results compared to unmodified battery cells.

Eight Li-ion battery cell experiments were conducted over two separate experimental series (4 cells tested in each series) that featured the same experimental configuration and battery parameters. The only difference in the series is the application of heating rates due to two modes of power delivery to the copper slug, which results in “slow heating” (Series 1) and “fast heating” (Series 2). The first series of experiments placed the copper slug on a temperature-controlled hot plate that was preheated to 420 °C. The total contact area of the bottom of the copper slug to the hot plate is 25.7 cm2. Heating rates were found to be varied between each cell, ranging from 3.8 °C/min to 8.2 °C/min. “Fast heating” was obtained by replacing the hot plate approach with two resistance band heaters clamped onto the outer shell of the copper cell holder. Each heater is 50 mm in diameter x 30 mm in height and consists of an outer stainless-steel shell surrounding a ceramic casing that houses a high-resistance wire. Each heater supplies 255 W, measured using a high current AC RMS voltage and current transducer. The power supply to the heaters is controlled using a PID temperature controller with a setpoint of 200 °C. Feedback to the controller is provided using one of the thermocouples inserted into the borehole. The PID controller was tuned to obtain a 100 % duty cycle, and constant power was delivered until thermal runaway in the battery cell occurred. The latter heating methodology enabled repeatable and controllable heating rates compared to the hot plate approach. In both heating cases, the outer shell of the cylindrical copper cell holder is covered with low-density mineral wool to create an adiabatic boundary condition. Only the top surface of the cylindrical cell holder is left exposed to air.

The arrangement of optical measurements employed is shown in Fig. 1b. High-speed imaging of broadband luminescence was undertaken using a monochromatic Photron Fastcam SA-X2 coupled to a 105 mm, f # = 4.5, UV Nikkor lens (Series 2 only) and a Chronos 1.4 high-speed colour camera coupled to a 12–75 mm f # = 1.2 Computar lens (Series 1 and Series 2). The viewing window of the Photron camera was set to 170 mm above the battery exit vent, with the camera gate set to 2.5 μs with a resolution of 1024 × 512 pixels. Images were acquired between 9 and 12 kHz, depending on the test. The Chronos high-speed colour camera had a larger viewing window of 0–250 mm above the battery exit vent, with camera gates ranging from 12.5 to 100 μs, and images acquired at either 1.5 kHz or 3.5 kHz, depending on the test. Both cameras were operated in a ring-buffer mode with a manual end trigger to capture the entirety of the thermal runaway sequence.

Time-resolved spectral emission measurements were taken with a Czerny-Turner CCD spectrometer (Thorlabs CCS200) with an effective spectral range from 400 to 1000 nm. The spectrometer is coupled to an optical fibre bundle (Thorlabs BFL200HS02), with light emission focused at 10 mm above the battery exit plane using a 50 mm, f # = 1.4 Canon lens, yielding a projected probe volume size of approximately 5 mm. Depending on the test, the acquired spectra are integrated between 50 and 100 ms, which is then corrected in post-processing to enable comparisons between cases.

High-speed light emission measurements were collected using a high-speed silicon photodiode detector (Thorlabs PDA100A2), with a spectral range of 200–1100 nm, positioned 410 mm from the battery, and a full 180° field-of-view. The voltage response from the photodiode is recorded on a digital storage oscilloscope (Tektronics TDS2014C). The photodiode also triggers a sound level meter (Brüel & Kjær Type 2250), which records the temporal evolution of the acoustic emission generated during the thermal runaway process. The sound level meter is positioned 350 mm from the battery cell, which enables the correlation of the high-speed light emission captured using the photodiode and the colour camera. Sound emissions have previously been used with high-speed imaging diagnostics to identify ignition and flame surface annihilation events in turbulent jet flames [37]. However, their application for analysing ignition from the ejected effluent of Li-ion batteries is seldom employed.

Eight 18650 NMC-811 cells were brought into thermal runaway through external heating of the copper cell holder. Before each test, cells were charged to 100 %, with the final voltage measured. The cell's initial and final masses were also measured and shown in Table 1 .

Temperatures at varying radial depths within the copper were within 1 °C of each other. Thus, it was assumed that the aluminium-encased cell was in thermal equilibrium with the much heavier copper cell holder. Fig. 2 shows the temperature-time history for each cell, which ranges from 3.8 °C/min - 8.2 °C/min for Series 1, which is quite variable. Considerably shorter and constant heating rates of approximately 19 °C/min were obtained using the 510 W electrical resistance band heaters in Series 2. There is an initial temperature lag at the start of each test as the copper cell holder must first reach thermal equilibrium. In all tests, the early safety venting event occurred between 150 °C–160 °C [2]. The safety venting process is characterised by an audible “pop” sound, which masks the puncture of the safety cap, followed by visible gas venting consisting of an aerosolised mixture.

Due to the prolonged heating rates for Series 1, the early gas venting had dissipated before thermal runaway. However, for Series 2, venting was still present at the instant of thermal runaway. In the latter case, the temperature rise rate slightly decreases as enthalpy from the aerosolised gas is released from the cell. Thermal runaway occurred for all cells, with the critical temperature and final cell mass listed in Table 1. Thermal runaway occurs at a very consistent temperature range between 170 and 180 °C, and the imposed heating rate appears to have little effect on this critical temperature. These thermal runaway temperatures are consistent with other studies using similar cathodes [44]. However, the total mass loss from each cell widely differed, ranging from 15.18g to 29.24 g (from an initial nominal cell mass of 42 g). Total flame/spark heights were measured to be 5 m above the battery exit plane. The initiation sequence of thermal runaway was found to be highly variable in each experiment after the opening of the burst disk and dominated by different local timescales. In some experiments (e.g. S1-C1, S2-C1, S1-C4, S2-C4), thermal runaway is initiated by the comparatively slow release of sparks, resulting in a spark shower that ramps up in intensity over 40 ms. However, other experiments (S1-C2, S2-C2, S2-C3) show a far more energetic initiation sequence with flames and glowing particles both ejected from the exit plane of the cell that nearly saturates the camera. Both observations of hot particles and flames emanating at the instant of thermal runaway are consistent with previous experimental observations [32]. However, the following sections will reveal the temporal and spatial structure of the ejecta.

ce:section.ce:section-title: High-speed imaging of particle-driven ignition modes
ce:section.ce:para:
Distinct ignition and flame propagation events are identified from the high-speed imaging measurements. Flames consistently form where dense particle fields or large fragments are observed. Instances of flame development in the near field region (0–170 mm) of the battery cell, followed by flame propagation, are observed in all eight tests. Five selected time sequences are presented in Fig. 3 that highlight the three dominant ignition modes that are repeatedly identified from all tests. The images are extracted from the Photron high-speed camera, and the time step between each image is shown. Each image is corrected for background noise, and a false colour map is imposed with a constant scale for all five sequences to highlight changes in the luminosity of the emission. The sequences showing ignition are extracted from Series 2 (S2) experiments, showing that flaming ignition of the gaseous effluent is driven by the presence of hot metal particles, consistent with prior research [12,30]. However, high-speed images across all experiments show that three distinct modes occur, which can be summarised as:
•
Mode 1: Ignition and jet flame development in a dense hot particle-laden shower (Fig. 3a)
•
Mode 2: Localised ignition on a singular small ejected solid fragment, resulting in a hot spot acting as an ignition kernel for the gaseous effluent (Fig. 3b–c).
•
Mode 3: Localised ignition on a comparatively larger ejected cylindrical shell originating from the cell jellyroll. The flame spreads over the shell, igniting regions of the gaseous effluent (Fig. 3d–e).

All three ignition modes are repeatedly observable in all tests; however, the sequences shown in Fig. 3 are exemplars extracted to illustrate the ignition modes, which serve as the basis for the subsequent discussion.

Mode 1 is shown in Fig. 3a, which is taken from S2-C1. In this mode, a hot stream of particles is initially released from the cell exit plane. Noticeable increases in the particle density downstream occur at 462 ms, and two distinct flame ignition kernels are identified at 100 mm and 140 mm on both sides of the central jet in the presence of both small and large particles. These ignition kernels develop into a merged flame structure at 466 ms, with a flame pocket propagating upstream. The expansion and upward propagation of the flame produces a luminous axisymmetric shear-layer jet flame structure that completely advects out of frame by 478 ms. Subsequent ignitions at 70 mm are identified at 478 ms, which results in further flame propagation.

Mode 2 is highlighted in Fig. 3b–c, extracted from S2-C2 and S2-C3, respectively, showing large isolated non-luminous fragments ejected at high velocities from the cell in the presence of a luminous glowing particles. The luminous flame allows for the visualisation of fragments in the flaming area due to the elevated contrast with the darker fragments. Fragments are highlighted with the arrows and a bounding box. Localised ignition on the non-luminous fragments can be observed on the outer edges at 216 ms (Figs. 3b) and 212 ms (Fig. 3c), respectively. At the next time step, luminous flames propagate over the entire fragment, and parts of the gas stream outside the fragment location ignite. This results in a propagating flame front that primarily advects downstream in the following frames. After the flame has propagated beyond the camera viewing window, the central jet of particles can be observed from 0 to 60 mm downstream of the battery cell. These particles are relatively dilute in comparison and possess comparatively lower luminosity, indicating a lower temperature. Consequently, the reignition of the ejected gas stream is unable to occur.

Mode 3 is shown in Fig. 3d-e, extracted from S2-C2 and S2-C3, respectively, and shows large non-luminous cylindrical shells from the jelly-roll internal structure of the battery ejected from the battery cell. Localised ignition occurring on the edge of the shell occurs at least 20–50 mm downstream of the cell. Upon ignition, flames grow along the shell, resulting in two potential pathways: (1) ignition of the ejected gaseous effluent, resulting in the formation of a propagating flame that is stabilised on the fragment, or (2) localised flaming of the shell without gas-phase ignition occurring. The former pathway can be demonstrated in Fig. 3d, which shows the formation of a hotspot at 116 ms, with gas-phase ignition occurring at 118 ms. The propagating flame extends to the right side of the cell, and two flame fronts are observed. The first flame front propagates downwards at a speed consistent with the cylindrical shell and is stabilised on the fragment. The secondary flame front propagates upstream against the flow of ejected gases and has a comparatively longer burnout time. It is noted that the large flaming region events well downstream of the battery are undoubtedly strongly supported by the oxygen provided by the entrainment of ambient air. However, in the present study, it is not possible to delineate the importance of entrainment of ambient air and oxidiser produced by the battery decomposition in the near-battery flame region, as the entire experiment was conducted in a quiescent ambient air environment. The mechanism resulting in the observed fragment-driven auto-ignition process for both Mode 2 and Mode 3 will be discussed further in Section 3.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time-evolution of light and sound emission
ce:section.ce:para: The temporal evolution of light emission during thermal runaway, captured by the high-speed photodiode and camera, is presented in Fig. 4 a–b. The photodiodes are normalised by their maximum value, providing a temporally resolved view of the highly intermittent luminous ejecta and ignition events. Signal saturation corresponds to large flaming events, while smaller peaks are associated with spark releases and highly localised ignition. The gain on the photodiode was slightly adjusted between experiments to boost the dynamic range, however, saturation of the photodetector still occurred. This was optimised considerably during the S2 experiments, thus significant improvements in dynamic range were obtained. The results establish that the total thermal runaway process with luminous effluent release is short, lasting between 0.5 and 1.5 s. Although safety venting and thermal runaway temperatures are consistent across all tests, the disparity in the time-histories suggests that most of the heat release is chemically driven by flaming combustion, as the glowing sparks exhibit comparatively weaker emissions.

To provide insight into the elevated or saturated photodiode signals, the total light emission within a window from 0 to 170 mm above the battery exit plane is evaluated by spatially integrating the grayscale-converted pixel values for each frame captured on the Photron high-speed camera for the S2 experiments. The high quantum efficiency of the camera, despite short gate times (2.5 μs), allows for the flames to be adequately differentiated from the particles. Thus, a semi-quantitative comparison of total light emission within the camera viewing window is obtained and shown in Fig. 4b. The measurements are paired with the global sound pressure emission captured during thermal runaway, shown in Fig. 4c. Peaks in light emission on the camera and photodiode confirm that ignition, followed by flaming combustion, is responsible for the largest signals. Smaller peaks correspond to localised ignition events and are distinct from the slightly elevated background signal. The latter is attributed to the continuous ejecta that consists of low-intensity particles (appearing as sparks) and gas; thus, it can be assumed that the continuous ejecta release has comparatively smaller enthalpy than flaming combustion. The light emission intensity contains both magnitude and the total area occupied by flames. Based on this metric, it can be observed that S2-C2 is the most energetic test, followed by S2-C1, S2-C4, and S2-S3.

Peaks in the sound emission (Fig. 4c) correlate with the camera luminosity measurements, demonstrating that flaming ignition and propagation generate detectable pressure waves and are distinct events during the thermal runaway process. It is important to note that the sound pressure is a global quantity that measures pressure waves generated by flames across the entire axial length of the effluent release (∼3–5 m), whereas the integrated camera intensity measurements are limited to a window of 170 mm above the cell burst disc. Given that there are several instances where sound pressure is measured but light emission is not (e.g. S2-C2, S2-C3), it can be inferred that a large quantity of flaming ignition and propagation events occur downstream of 250 mm beyond the battery vent. Instances where the luminosity and pressure intensity ratio do not match (e.g. S2-C1) correspond to ignition, and flame propagation does not occur within the initial 170 mm window from the cell exit plane (that is visible to the camera) and establishes further downstream. This is confirmed by large field-of-view images captured by digital SLR camera imaging, which show flames propagating up to 5 m beyond the battery vent.

To demonstrate the correlation between the high-speed camera, photodiode and sound pressure, the signals for S2 are cross-correlated, with the maximum normalised cross-correlation coefficients (
ρ
x y
) shown in Table 2 for each test.

Across all experiments, the correlation between the photodiode and high-speed camera is strong (ranging between 0.73 and 0.92), reinforcing that the peaks in the photodiode signal correspond to luminous ignition events, which are intermittent and distinct from the continuous spark releases. The correlation to the sound pressure is moderately strong for S2-C1 to S2-C3, but weak for S2-C4. This appears to be driven by significant sound emission captured towards the end of thermal runaway, which could be associated with multiple smaller ignitions, noise or directional effects. However, the initial peaks in sound correlate with distinct luminous ignition events. Thus, ignition and flaming of the ejecta generate a reliably detectable pressure wave.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visible spectral signature and ignition mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Time-resolved optical spectral emissions measured at a probe volume located 5 mm above the battery cell exit plane region were undertaken to identify the dominant atomic emissions from the particles during thermal runaway. Measurements were taken for all experiments. However, for clarity, S1-C2 is presented in Fig. 5 , which has the most distinct spectra from all the tests with good dynamic range, and is integrated over 100 ms. The data has been corrected for the spectral throughput of the spectrometer, fibre, and lens configuration using a broadband source (candle flame) and for the background noise.

A broad grey body distribution from 400 to 850 nm is obtained during the thermal runaway duration, with varying levels of intensity based on the test and time. In several instances, distinct atomic emission lines are identified in superposition with the broad grey body distribution. These lines are typically considerably greater than the grey body and background signal, and in some cases, the emission saturates the CCD detector. Major emission lines are identified at λ = 671.5 nm, corresponding to the 2p←2s lithium lines and at λ = 589.7 nm, corresponding to the sodium D-lines at the 3p←3s transition. The 2p←2s lithium line is the strongest spectral signal detected in all experiments and indicates that neutral lithium (Li I) is detected during distinct flaming events resulting from the combustion of the fragments ejected from the battery cell. Due to the low grating resolution of the spectrometer (600 lines/mm), neither the major twin lines of lithium nor sodium can be distinguished. Emission lines are also obtained at λ = 611.7 nm, 766.9 nm, and 770.2 nm, corresponding to the 2p←3d lithium line and the potassium 4p←4s lines, which are also superimposed onto the grey body distribution.

A weak continuous broad emission is present during the entirety of thermal runaway, which correlates with the release of hot metal sparks observed from high-speed imaging with both the colour and monochromatic cameras. During the intermittent ignition events, the continuous grey body distribution increases substantially, and is often paired with the detection of atomic emission. The measured continuous spectra fit the shape of a Planck distribution, with the peak emission in the infrared beyond the range of the spectrometer. It should be noted that the high-speed imaging reveals that ignition often occurs downstream of the spectrometer measurement location, suggesting that the atomic emission is correlate with the occurance of flaming combustion.

The strong spectral response of lithium at 671 nm indicates that the high-temperature luminous flaming ejecta contains neutral lithium (Li I). It is important to note that the lithium ions (Li+) present in the cathode, anode or electrolyte will rapidly react to form stable lithium-containing molecules or form neutral-state lithium metal (Li I) during thermal runaway before being ejected from the battery. Hence, emission from Li+ (also termed Li II) is not expected to occur, and Li II transitions are not observed in the spectral data. Intermediate molecular decomposition products such as LiF and compounds normally found in the battery, such as LiFP6, LiCoO2, LiMnO2, and LiNiO2 that contain the Li + cation, are not pyrophoric. It is believed that Li I originates from (1) metallic lithium plating on the graphite anode surface during charging (particularly at 100 % SOC, as in these experiments) or (2) reductive decomposition of lithiated graphite (LiC6) during thermal runaway. Metallic lithium plating occurs under kinetic limitations (e.g., high SOC, fast charging, low temperatures), depositing reactive Li I on the anode surface [45]. Oxidation of plated lithium is highly exothermic [46], explaining the observed pyrophoric combustion of ejected fragments from the jellyroll (containing the anode) upon air exposure that was interpreted as auto-ignition in Section 3.1. Lithium formation may occur due to the delithiation of intercalated lithium ions at high SOC, which results from thermal decomposition of the anode and SEI during heating [47]. The decomposed products of LiC6 released from the exposed interior of the anode can result in highly reactive residual lithium leached over the surface of the graphite anode [48], which again enables a pathway for observed pyrophoric combustion of the large anode fragments ejected from the cell and the detection of visible Li I atomic emission. It should also be noted that LiC6 itself is highly reactive with oxygen and is a key driver in thermal runaway [49]. The results suggest that gaseous ignition of the effluent gases above the cell requires a strong pilot, which may be provided by the pyrophoric ignition of anode fragments containing lithium ejected from the cell.

The velocity field of the ejecta during thermal runaway is a key parameter that defines the overall energetics and the heat transfer that may be imposed on neighbouring cells in a module. Moreover, in the context of ignition, the local velocity or strain rate has classically been the key variable controlling ignition delay times for turbulent auto-igniting jet flames. Thus far, no data on the ejecta velocity fields during the entirety of thermal runaway have been reported beyond the incipient stages [36]. To extract the velocity field, the image sequences are post-processed using cross-correlations of the ejecta field. No laser sheet is used; therefore, the velocity fields presented are integrated along the line of sight. This is not expected to impact the vertical component velocities (u-component) greatly due to the strong axial components observed owing to the removal of the vent cap, but it may bias the radial (v-component) due to the out-of-plane motion.

Images from the Photron SA-X2 camera for Series 2 experiments are analysed frame-by-frame to simulate Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) measurements. Cross-correlations of the particle-laden ejecta and the resulting velocity fields were computed using PIVLab v2.63 [50], which is an open-source code. Images have a physical dimension of 164 × 82 mm2 with a projected pixel size of 160 μm/pixel. A triple-pass approach is used for the interrogation window size, starting from 128 × 128 pixels and moving to 32 × 32 pixels, with 50 % overlap. Background corrections, a 3 × 3 median filter and brightness thresholding are applied to boost the signal-to-noise ratio and remove spurious signals such as large flames that saturate the CMOS sensor. Interrogation windows where particles are not detected or do not pass the validation criterion are filtered out from the resulting statistics and velocity fields.

Fig. 6 shows contours displaying ensemble mean and RMS velocity magnitudes at sequential time windows for an exemplar case: S2-C1. Each time window spans 0.105 s (1000 shots), with velocity windows presented from 0.21 to 0.32 s, 0.32–0.43 s, 0.43–0.54 s, 0.65–0.77 s, 0.88–0.99 s, 1.27–1.38 s, and 1.43–1.54 s. The directional components of the mean velocity field are highlighted by the arrows displayed on each contour. The analysis demonstrates a highly intermittent velocity field that changes in intensity. The time evolution of the velocity field mirrors the light and sound emission results shown earlier. A distinct central jet structure is identified in all windows, except towards the end of the thermal runaway period, as the heat release rate decreases. However, this structure is driven by the removal of the vent cap, which produces orthogonal jets as it acts as a deflector plate for the flow. However, the flow still retains a jet structure [14,38]. As cross-correlations of bulk particles in the flow form the basis for the analysis, conditioning the velocities based on particle size is impossible and will be addressed in future measurements.

The largest velocities are found inside the core of the jet, which can range from 15 to 25 m s−1. These periods are most notable from 0.21 to 0.43 s and 0.88–0.99 s and consist of intense particulate ejection of dense hot, luminous metal sparks, jellyroll fragments, and ignition events. Particle velocities in the highly energetic phases are not the greatest at the exit plane of the battery cell, but accelerate further downstream between 50 and 100 mm before de-accelerating. The vectors in the central jet possess a strong axial component. It should be noted that large particle velocities are intrinsically linked to occurrences of flaming, which may seem counterintuitive due to smaller residence times of the gas phase, thus supporting the notion that large particle number densities are critical to enabling ignition. Velocities are greatest in periods where the number density of fragments is large, and the results indicate that the expansion of hot gases due to flame propagation accelerates the particles in the local direction of the flame front. This presumes that a significant component of particles is small and thus follows the bulk flow field. However, this cannot be verified without particle sizing measurements. RMS velocities in all time windows are numerically larger than the mean quantities, indicating significant velocity fluctuations during the thermal runaway process. However, given the similar structure of the mean and RMS field, the fluctuations appear to be attributed to bulk flow from the battery cell rather than turbulent fluctuations found in jet flames. A large brush area occupied by high RMS velocity particles is identified, which reflects the continual shifting of the bulk particle ejection cone angle.

Between sequences of highly energetic releases, regions of comparatively dilute particle releases with no ignition events are prevalent, as observed in the window from 0.65 to 0.77 s. The particles exhibit low luminosity and possess a narrow jet structure. Mean velocities are also uniform inside the jet, between 10 and 13 m s−1, with the RMS velocity slightly larger at 15–17 m s−1. The end of the thermal runaway process is marked by a substantial decrease in the particle velocity and number density at 1.26–1.47 s. Mean velocities in the centre part of the jet range between 6 and 7 m s−1 and 2–3 m s−1 towards the edge of the particle brush. Velocities decay dramatically past 75 mm above the battery cell but exhibit a preferential release towards one side. This is suspected due to molten slag solidifying at the ruptured burst disc location, thereby affecting the pathway for particle release from the battery cell. This is often observed towards the end of thermal runaway across numerous experiments, where the solidification of molten slag on the battery cell exit plane is observed in conjunction with a reduction in particle luminosity and, therefore, energetics. In this case, the radial velocity components reach a point consistent with the axial velocity. RMS velocities are also consistent in the particle release area between 9 and 10.5 m s−1 (1.26–1.37 s) before decaying to 7–8 m s−1 (1.37–1.47 s). The obstruction in the orifice area at the opened burst disc becomes observable by the distinct lack of particles on one side of the cell (−20 mm < x < 0 mm).

Probability density functions (PDFs) of the axial (u) and radial (v) component velocities are extracted from the PIV analysis for Series 2 experiments in Fig. 7 . To eliminate any influence from out-of-plane motion on the statistics, both velocity components are conditioned on the velocity magnitude,
v →
> 0.5 m s−1. Conditioning based on the magnitude eliminates recorded particles that appear stationary due to non-planar motion in the images. The velocity components are collected from the entire spatial and temporal window, capturing the thermal runaway process. Despite variances in the time series of the ejecta emission (Fig. 4), the PDFs of velocity distributions converge towards the overall duration shown in Fig. 7, and thus, only ensemble statistics are shown.

PDFs of the conditional u-component velocity in Fig. 7 demonstrate a similar log-normal distribution, with shifts in broadening between tests. Based on the test, the maximum conditional axial velocities vary between 30 and 35 m s−1. In particular, S2-C1 and S2-C3 possess broader PDFs, with larger u-component velocities obtained. Despite these minor differences in the log-normal distribution, consistent statistical distributions of the entire thermal runaway process are attained, regardless of the unique time history observed from the high-speed diagnostics for each test. Negative axial velocities are identified despite the high inertia of the ejecta. However, this is attributed to the rapid expansion of flames that drives some of the particles downwards towards the battery cell due to the propagating turbulent flame front. Examples of a flame front propagating upstream are shown earlier during several of the ignition sequences. The conditional v-component velocity PDFs are consistent between the tests, with a Gaussian PDF that is centred about zero. This is likely due to the removal of the vent cap, which eliminates the possibility of preferential venting due to molten slag blocking some of the vent holes, and thus, a broader distribution would be expected in untampered 18650 cells. Radial velocities upwards of 10 m s−1 on both sides of the cell can be obtained. These velocities are more prevalent on the edges of the particle ejection cone. These results highlight that the key physical variables underpinning the ejected particle-laden flow from Li-ion battery cells in thermal runaway (e.g. velocity and scalar fields) collapse into a statistical distribution. High sample rate diagnostics are key tools that provide the necessary temporal resolution to adequately capture the fast and highly intermittent nature of changes in these key variables.

This paper introduces a new experimental rig and optical diagnostic platform to investigate the time-resolved effluent ejection and ignition dynamics of single 18650 NMC Li-ion cells during thermal runaway. Cells at 100 % SOC were externally heated, using constant and variable heating rates, until thermal runaway occurred between 170 °C–180 °C. Broadband high-speed luminescence imaging, visible spectral imaging, high-speed photodetection, and sound pressure measurements were employed to capture the complete temporal history of the thermal runaway process in the near-field of the battery cell vent. Key findings from this experimental study include:
•
A new ignition mode is proposed, where flaming ignition of the unburned flammable gaseous effluent ejected above the cell is driven by the pyrophoric combustion of large ejecta fragments containing neutral lithium (Li I). The detection of strong Li I atomic emission at 671 nm suggests this lithium originates from metallic lithium plating or delithiated graphite (LiC6) on the anode that is exposed during thermal runaway. Larger fragments can travel significant distances before ignition occurs. While ignition due to broadband metal sparks is possible, it requires a higher spark density due to the lower relative temperature than ignited fragments.
•
Ejecta velocity fields during the entire thermal runaway process have been explicitly measured with high temporal resolution and are largest in the central ejecta jet. Peak velocities (>25 m s−1) correlate with peaks in the flame luminosity and sound pressure. Conditional axial velocity distributions exhibit a log-normal profile, ranging from −2 m s−1 to 35 m s−1, while conditional radial velocities show a Gaussian distribution centred near 0 m s−1, due to the removal of the vent cap.
•
Intense atomic emission of Li I, Na, and K in the flaming ejecta from an NMC Li-ion battery cell under thermal runaway has been identified and is superimposed onto a continuous broad grey body distribution.
•
Thermal runaway is a transient, highly intermittent process with consistent failure temperatures but large variability in emission intensity, ejection behaviour, and ignition events. Peak emission and flame development occur at the onset of thermal runaway and subsides rapidly within 0.5–2.0 s.
•
Flaming events generate significant sound pressure waves that correlate with light emission peaks. Continuous particle and spark emissions produce weaker grey body radiation.

These results underscore the complex dynamics governing effluent ejection during thermal runaway and highlight the necessity for high spatial and temporal resolution measurements. The data acquired are crucial for developing robust statistical models to capture the intermittent nature of thermal runaway at the single-cell level. Future work will focus on further improving these techniques, employing joint high-speed filtered imaging on a large sample size of cells to build a statistical database.
